D3 Chemistry L5 Evaluate Reference

UV Photodegradation — Bond Cleavage Kinetics

a UV photon delivers enough energy to break a covalent bond; chromophore lifetime follows τ = 1/(σ·φ·I) — the same equation predicts how long a watercolour lasts in a sunlit hallway

UV at 350 nm carries ~3.5 eV per photon — enough to homolytically cleave a typical C-C bond (~3.6 eV). The rate at which an organic pigment fades depends on the absorption cross-section σ at UV wavelengths, the quantum yield φ of bond breaking, and the photon flux I. The product σ·φ·I is the inverse lifetime: stronger UV absorber = faster fade.

Drag UV intensity. Three pigments age in parallel — alizarin (low UV abs, slow), Prussian blue (medium), Sap green (high UV abs, fast). After N years the swatches show retained colour fraction.
